title: ASX "sleepwalking" in response to events in the Middle East, says Matthew Haupt | The Business
description: The  Australian share market has shrugged off the risks of the widening war in the Middle East, paring the day's earlier losses.  Brent crude futures surged by 12 per cent at the open on fears the war will choke global oil supplies, but at the close of Australia's trading day, was up 6.5 percent. The price of safe haven asset, gold rose by 1.5 per cent. The energy sector was the highest performing asset. Wilson Asset Management lead portfolio manager Matthew Haupt says the muted reaction means he doesn't think the ASX is taking into account the seriousness of the events in the Middle East. He says Australia is 'sleepwalking' at the moment, and is likely to get a 'real wake up call'. #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. description: Economists have warned Australians war in the Middle East could see a surge in petrol prices, as much as 30c per litre, as the conflict disrupts oil trade through the Strait of Hormuz. There have also been concerns of further-reaching economic impacts if the conflict were to drag on. title: ASX rattled slightly by Iran strikes, oil price jumps, banks tumble | Finance Report | ABC NEWS
description: The local share market was flat today after war broke out in the Middle East, despite bigger falls on global markets. Oil prices jumped as shipping in the region was disrupted, lifting local energy stocks. Gold miners and supermarkets also rose as investors looked for safe havens. Bank shares fell as investors worried about private credit businesses and their ties to major banks. title: House prices rise as battlelines are drawn on changes to investor tax breaks | The Business
description: House prices continued their upward trajectory in February, in spite of the latest interest rate rise. Property prices have risen 0.8 per cent in February and 9.9 per cent over the year. The national median home value is now more than $920,000. Data from property insight firm Cotality reveals Perth is leading the charge followed by Brisbane and Adelaide then Hobart, the regions, Canberra, Darwin, Sydney and Melbourne. Gerard Burg from Cotality says there's been a lot more activity in the lower parts of the market, both around the country on the urban fringes of major cities, but also into regional areas.  "A lot of buyers are seeking the opportunity for more affordable housing," he says It comes as battle lines are drawn over the capital gains tax discount for property investors. Treasurer Jim Chalmers has opened the door to winding back the 50 per cent discount in the May federal budget, saying they are "alive to the intergenerational issues in the house market and in the tax system". While the Opposition leader Angus Taylor says it won't increase supply. This comes as a Senate inquiry is wrestling with whether reducing the government's 50 per cent Capital Gains Tax Discount when investors sell, will make it easier for first home buyers. #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE Read more here:  https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-02/house-prices-auction-data-cotality/106395008  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Iran lashes out at Gulf states as Trump says operation could last weeks | ABC NEWS
description: After the stunning confirmation of the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khomeini, the US claims to have killed nearly 50 other Iranian leaders. US President Donald Trump says the fighting in the Gulf could last for weeks and has vowed revenge after the first confirmed American deaths in the war.  00:00 Israel and the US have intensified their attacks on Tehran. 01:02 Israel have responded to an attack by Hezbollah, attacking Lebanon. 01:15 Iran has struck neighbouring Gulf states, including luxury hotels in Dubai. 01:34 Israel has received retaliatory strikes, including a residential area where nine were killed. 02:13 The Australian government has said it is not involved with the strikes, but supports the objective. 03:13 Middle East Correspondent Matthew Doran reports from Beit Shemesh and how the conflict could still escalate. 04:12 US Correspondent Kamin Gock reports on the first American casualties from the conflict and the political response in America. 06:32 ABC Correspondent Ben Knight looks at how the Iranian regime has responded to the attacks and asks how it can possibly survive. title: Opposition leader claims women, children in Syrian camp are 'ISIS sympathisers' | ABC NEWS
description: The Coalition has branded a group of Australian children as terrorist "sympathisers" because their mothers travelled to Syria and married Islamic State militants, and argued the federal government should shut the door on their return.  Labor insists it has not assisted the cohort's attempted exit and is simply complying with the law in issuing travel documents to Australian citizens. An imminent second attempt to leave the Al Roj camp is unlikely as conflict in the Middle East has shut down airspace.  The Coalition has also questioned why temporary exclusion orders (TEOs) have not been applied to some of the children among the group of 34 Australians seeking to return.  This cohort includes 23 children aged between six and 18, as well as 11 adult women.  Mr Taylor repeatedly called the group "ISIS sympathisers" and did not separate the underage children from that description when asked to clarify if he was including them.  Shadow Home Affairs Minister Jonno Duniam suggested TEOs should be applied to the underage Australians, who he described as "so-called children". title: Hezbollah enters Middle East conflict, Israel launches retaliatory strikes | ABC NEWS
description: People have been seen fleeing Lebanon's capital Beirut on foot and by car, after Israel launched retaliatory strikes against Iran-aligned Lebanese militia Hezbollah.  The Israeli military issued a warning ordering residents of dozens of villages in southern and eastern Lebanon to evacuate ahead of the strikes.  It comes after Hezbollah launched missiles and drones towards Israel in response to the killing of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.   Lebanese government officials had urged the Iran-backed group not to enter the conflict, but Hezbollah claims its strikes are a legitimate act of self-defence.  Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has reiterated Australia's concerns about an escalation in the conflict.  The federal government says there are around 115,000 Australians in the region, including more than 100 defence force personnel.  Australians in Iran, Israel, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates are being urged to register with the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade. title: Iran security chief Ali Larijani likely to be next supreme leader | The World | ABC NEWS
description: In the aftermath of the death of Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, attention has turned to who will succeed him. Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council — tasked with a wide remit in Iran's constitution to preserve the Islamic Revolution, territorial integrity and national sovereignty — has emerged as a frontrunner to lead the Islamic Republic. Iran-born journalist and Middle East expert, Tara Kangarlou spoke to The World's Girish Sawlani about what lies ahead for the future of the Iranian regime. title: Can Iran's Revolutionary Guards withstand US-Israeli strikes? | The World | ABC NEWS
description: As Israel and the United States continue striking targets within Iranian territory, the pillar of Iran's military, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, has warned it's going to unleash its greatest offensive operation. US President Donald Trump has called on IRGC troops to lay down their arms or face certain death. Middle East expert Adel Abdel Ghafar spoke to The World's Girish Sawlani about what lies ahead for the Iran's military and how much support can it get from militia groups, including Lebanon's Hezbollah and Yemen's Houthis. title: Gulf states flag response to Iran's 'heinous attacks' | The World | ABC NEWS
description: Iran has fired missiles at targets in nine countries in the region. They include the states that comprise the Gulf Cooperation Council - the UAE, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar and Kuwait, who have hinted they could launch their own strikes on Iran. They've issued a statement vowing to defend themselves against Iranian attacks "including the option of responding to the aggression". The World's Girish Sawlani spoke to Professor Amnon Aran, middle east expert from the University of London about the implications of a broader regional conflict. title: Critical oil route throttled by Iran conflict | The World | ABC NEWS
description: The war in the Middle East has caused global oil prices to surge. Iran has one of the world's largest oil reserves and has the power to effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical oil trade route. Brent crude jumped 10% when energy markets opened for the week, but later settled to sit at just below 80 USD a barrel. Jorge León from Rystad Energy spoke to The World's Girish Sawlani about the impact of these developments on the energy industry.
